Product background and overview

spyc 🌶️

The file commander built for collaborating
with your coding agents.

pronounced spy-see, a.k.a. spicy. keyboard-driven · MCP-native · Rust · macOS + Linux

Derek Marshall Tripstack · Etraveli Group · 2026

What is spyc?

A two-pane terminal program in the lineage of keyboard-driven file commanders: a vi-flavoured file commander on top, your coding agent below (Claude Code by default; codex, Gemini, Antigravity, zot also supported). They share a local MCP socket, so the agent sees exactly what you're looking at.

The problem

  • "AI in your terminal" gives the agent a chat window and copy-pasted paths
  • The agent can't see what you're looking at
  • "Which file?" gets asked every other turn
  • Flipping between a file browser and the agent kills flow

The answer

  • File list and agent pane in one session
  • The agent asks the socket: what's the cursor on, what's staged, what's picked
  • Bidirectional — gf jumps from agent output back to the file list
  • Session restore resumes each pane to its own agent conversation

The MCP bridge

What makes spyc more than a terminal inside a terminal.

How it works

  • An MCP server on a PID-scoped Unix socket
  • Agents auto-discover it — .mcp.json (Claude), .codex/config.toml (codex), and the rest, written on launch
  • spyc --mcp re-execs as a stdio proxy to the one socket
  • Multiple instances coexist; takeover is prompted
  • Hand-rolled JSON-RPC — no async runtime

What the agent sees, and drives

  • Read: cwd, cursor, picks, inventory, filter, git branch
  • Search: search_paths, search_content (gitignore-aware, in-process)
  • Git: git_status / git_log / git_diff
  • Drive: navigate_to, set_filter, pick_files, get_file_content

"Filter to Rust files and pick the tests" → the agent calls set_filter + pick_files → the TUI updates live.

Which agent needs my attention

Run several agents across tabs and worktrees and the real question becomes which one is waiting on me. Each agent reports its own state, so spyc can tell you.

Activity dots

Each pane tab carries a live dot — working / blocked / done — self-reported by the agent over the MCP/hook channel, with an output-timing fallback. Stays right even when the agent redraws its UI.

Notifications

On the transition into blocked or done: a desktop ping and a spice-heat border pulse (the visual bell), so it reaches you in another window. Intrusive channels stay blocked-only by default.

Scope registry

register_scope / wait_for_scope_clear keep parallel agents from merging the same files at once — advisory, in-process, no daemon. Session-persisted across -r.

See it at a glance in the tab bar, and get a desktop ping the moment an agent starts waiting on you.

The best of both worlds didn't exist

I live in a file commander, and I pair with a coding agent. Both lineages have excellent tools — but each owns one lane, and nothing put them in one place. So I started building spyc.

Agent managers

herdr, Claude Squad, cmux — great at running many agents across panes and worktrees.

What I still wanted: a file manager to live in, review built in, and an MCP bridge so the agent sees my view.

File managers

Yazi and the Rust TUI file managers — fast, polished, a joy to navigate.

Built before coding agents, though — no pairing, no MCP bridge.

spyc is the one I wanted — a real file manager, wired to your agents, with review built in.

Full landscape: docs/COMPETITIVE_REVIEW.md.

By the numbers · as of July 2026

77K
Lines of Rust
203
Source files
1,590
Tests
1,467
Commits
25
MCP tools
5
Agent profiles
100%
In-process git (gix)
0
Async runtime

Dogfooded. spyc is both a product of Claude Code and a host for it. Nearly every commit was written while pairing with an agent (Claude, codex, gemini) in spyc's own pane — a git-marker bug even turned up while this deck was being edited. A scope registry keeps concurrent agents from colliding.

Guardrails. The invariants that matter are enforced as tests: no git subprocess in production, a size ceiling on mod.rs, the binding-tier taxonomy, doc and module-index sync, a comment-hygiene check. Break one and CI goes red.

Architecture. Model-View-Update, with a pure domain, a pure render pass, and effects as data. It runs synchronously throughout, with no async runtime. Everything builds under clippy pedantic and nursery with warnings as errors, unwrap is denied in the pure layers, and there's one unsafe block in the whole codebase.

ratatui · crossterm · vt100 · portable-pty · gix · mlua — static musl Linux binaries, macOS universal.

Architecture — MVU, sync, one loop

Three disjoint state types: state (Model, pure domain), runtime (OS handles), view (render). Effects are data; one executor touches the OS.

src/app/MVU: state/ (Model), render/ (pure), effect.rs, command_table, key_dispatch — ~40 child modules
src/keymap/Action enum (full verb vocabulary + tiers), resolver, keymap DSL
src/pane/Pty subprocess, multi-tab, vt100 rendering, key encoding
src/mcp/Unix-socket MCP server + stdio proxy, per-agent config, status hooks
src/agent/Per-agent profiles (claude/codex/gemini/agy/zot); one impl + one registry entry
src/git/100% in-process gix — status, diff/show/blame, worktrees (no subprocess)
src/lua/Embedded Lua (mlua) on an off-thread worker with a kill switch
src/ui/ · state/ · fs/Pure renderers · cursor/marks/picks/inventory/graveyard/sessions · listing, finder, grep

Every action is an Action variant + a keymap binding + a handler arm. Guard tests keep the boundaries honest.

Design patterns

MVU / Elm

Model / Runtime / View split. Pure transitions return Effects as data; run_effects is the only OS executor. Render is pure &self. Unit-testable end to end.

Dirty-frame render

Event-driven: 0 draws-per-second at idle. DEC 2026 synchronized output wraps every frame; rows/grid cached by a generation counter.

In-process gix

Git is 100% in-process — no subprocess, guard-enforced. In-house syntax-highlighted diff / show / blame, side-by-side or unified.

Agent status, self-reported

Each agent reports its own state over the MCP/hook channel, with a tunable scrape fallback for the ones that can't report.

Off-thread everything

Blocking work (git walk, file ops, previews, Lua) runs on detached threads that wake the loop with a Message. The Model never blocks.

Vi everywhere

Modal navigation from the file list to the pager to the shell prompt to the Lua-scriptable keymap. One muscle memory.

Feature highlights

File commander

  • Vi motions, marks, picks (per-dir), inventory (persistent cache)
  • Graveyard — compressed, undo-able soft-delete recovery
  • Harpoon pins, = glob filter, fuzzy finder F, :grep
  • Git-status gutter, in-house diff/show/blame (gd/gD/gu)

Agent pane

  • Multi-tab pty; MCP bridge (read + drive)
  • Activity dots + desktop / visual-bell notifications
  • gf/gF jump; ^a v scrollback; session resume per agent
  • Worktree MCP + scope registry for multi-agent work

Vertical split

  • Live-reloading file preview column
  • Or a full second file commander — own cwd/git/harpoon, worktree-scoped MCP

Extensible

  • .spycrc.toml DSL — themes, keymap, layout, live reload
  • Lua scripting — map KEY lua, init.lua, spyc.on events

The arc

The commander, wired to the agent
Split pane + the MCP context bridge — the thesis
MVU / Elm migration
Model/Runtime/View split; app/mod.rs decomposed (12.4k → ~1k)
git → gix
100% in-process; in-house diff/show/blame
Agent awareness
Activity dots, notifications, scope registry, session-id pinning
Lua · vsplit · worktree MCP · chords
Scripting platform, second commander, safe worktree teardown, which-key
v2.0 — public launch
Distribution pass; feature set is launch-ready

Road to 2.0

The feature set is launch-ready. Everything left for 2.0 is distribution: packaging, signing, and a public launch.

The only gate: distribution

  • Public on GitHub (Tripstack-Corp/spyc) — dev + CI already moved
  • Release pipeline + signed binaries (macOS notarized, Linux musl)
  • Homebrew tap, demo cast, migration page

Post-2.0 (in Issues)

  • Daily-driver polish, session forking, prompt templates
  • Backlog + roadmap now live in GitHub Issues + the board
  • ROADMAP.md keeps the strategy and the decisions log

See it in motion

Live demo

  • Open the agent, then pick three architecture docs in the commander
  • Ask about "these three docs" — never naming them; the agent reads your picks over MCP
  • It answers with a path; gf jumps the listing straight to that file

In-process review

Diff & blame — no git subprocess

gd opens the in-house side-by-side diff vs HEAD; | toggles unified.

Vertical split

A live preview beside the list

^a | cycles a right-hand column that renders the cursor file live.

Agent awareness

The dot tells you who needs you

two claude tabs; one stays busy ● while the other blocks for input — a red ■ and the visual bell say which one needs you.
